item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as it comes from here with no indication it has been released under Creative Commons. Thus, the image has been removed.

EDIT: The image that was used in this article has been replaced with the following image, which is cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0):

Source: PublicDomainPictures.net
License: CC0
Title: Old Wallpaper
Author: Kevin Casper
Release year: N/A

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not sure if a mistake or not but it's written that all personnel have to read incident report 1205-G█ but the incident report given is 1205-C█.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Countin' flowers on the wall
that don't bother me at all …

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This has the first character I have actually felt sorry for in an SCP article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I came to this little masterpiece via per page discussions, and haven't seen the name. If it's called Wallflower, you owe me an internet.

Edit: I AM FUCKING PSYCHIC.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow, it's been a while since I've read this one. Yeah, the author is using "redacted" as a slangy noun for "who knows where".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: hey when you say "E. isn't here today; off-site, somewhere in redacted" is the author writing redacted as in he wasn't told where or is the information being redacted? if its the second one wouldn't it be written like (REDACTED)?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Would the name of this happen to be a Bastion reference?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've seen this kind of thing before, but I like what you did with it here. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've reread it in light of the crit, and I don't think I can really cure the "this reminds me of other thing eh" characteristics.

But I have been working on a hopefully-not-dumb Foundation Tale connection, which is narrated from the point of view of Mr. [DATA REDACTED BY REQUEST]. Not sure if that fits the something-more bill you were describing, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Novote for now but I'll think about it. I like it but it feels like it needs one or two more things to connect the dots.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Scip" has been used as the slang pronunciation of "SCP" in several places. It's not my thing personally, but it's got history.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
*ffffffffff*
Dear god… That was just depressing, but in a good/creepy way. The item itself is good and weird, but then the incident log… Shivers, man. Shivers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
His appearance changed more than the scip's
scip

what lol

upvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's also a Richard Matheson short story called "Disappearing Act" that has a very similar premise and - like the SCP - is basically composed from entries of the main character's diary.

That said, I like the vibe of the article. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think by the time I hit that spot, I was sufficiently creeped out such that it didn't have an effect one way or the other.

Implying nothing, I'll say that it is the most Yellow Wallpaper-esque part of this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Both, and I was hung up on how to describe that— particularly how/if to note that the paper didn't seem to be shrinking or growing, but doing both at different times. But now I'm thinking an edit to square might make the most sense….

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
These changes have never added or subtracted more than six centimeters from the object's surface area.

Do you mean six square centimeters of area, or six centimeters of the perimeter moving in/out?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I guess it's a creep vs. uncertainty tradeoff. A matter of taste, of course, but I'd rather go with the uncertainty. The peeling bit seemed to erase the mystery for me, since it pretty much comes down on one side of the whose-perception-is-getting-fucked-with debate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I disagree, that creeped me out the most. The paper peeling gave me a serious case of the jeebies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you want to keep it that uncertain, take out the part about the junior researcher seeing the piece tear away.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, I am really glad that someone took that from it— because that was the element I thought worked pretty well. I wanted it to be uncertain how much the SCP was changing the researcher's perspective— and how much it was changing other people's. Plus the implication that you can't really predict who it will effect that way… and the possibility that her records could be gradually lost over time.

… I just wasn't sure how well that came across.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh man, I didn't even think of the Yellow Wallpaper until it was mentioned in the comments. I don't think she's quite crazy enough for that.

What I like about this is that it's basically anomalous wallpaper + journal of researcher fading out of existence. There's a link, but they're not sure the SCP is the cause, and that's where the creep factor comes in for this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ha, in my (slight) defense, the only thing it sparked was a search for images of old wallpaper. The nature of its effects is pretty different (…and this wasn't a feminist criticism of forced-relaxation psychiatric treatments).

Buuut since it's getting a few "reminds me of…" I'll have to work on that, in the future.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm pretty sure the only thing redacted is one person's identity, actually… but I can rephrase it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it a lot.
I voted before learning it was possibly subconscious use of a previous story, but, regardless, I like it.

Un-voting, but this is still good writing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, my mind is fully functional now. Like Gravity said, it is reminiscent of The Yellow Wallpaper. However, I think this is a legitimately good idea with good execution. Also, I would suggest less expunging and redaction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If I knew, I'd say. (Not intended as snark.) It's just…missing something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: oh jeez, when I found the image, I was trying to remember the name of this wallpaper story I read as a kid— and I think that's the one. I need to reread it, now…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hum. Reminds of The Yellow Wallpaper and that's not a good thing, but it's not a bad article.

No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Any particular part of the execution? Too much, too little of something— ?

I've considered revising the scenario.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh, I saw more similarity to that episode in the guy who had to keep reminding people that he existed.

I like the idea of this one — playing with the phrase 'fading into the wallpaper' — but the execution leaves much to be desired. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I haven't seen much of Buffy, but the episode you're describing (Out of Mind, Out of Sight?) turned up pretty fast on Google…

..ecch. Guess that confirms my worry that this is an oft-used conceit, if nothing else.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Feels like that episode of buffy, with the girl who turns invisible. Not realyl that interesting. Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm going to let it sit for a bit, before adding anything. I'm a little iffy over the clarity of the report…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I think that one works better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I see what you're doing, but I think you haven't implied quite enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: yup, I can do that. I have another draft that… makes some reference to the redactions, so I didn't want to imply too much. The first one can probably be excised altogether— I'll have to think about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The testimony of her [REDACTED BY REQUEST]

[REDACTED BY REQUEST] and further notes

We need a few more words brought out of redaction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Weird regenerating wallpaper. I'm not quite sure what to think about this one right now. Maybe once I've gotten some sleep…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Criticism on the length, style, clarity, and effectiveness of the "excerpts" would be especially appreciated. I cut out a few sections (including one addressing its "Safe" status) because I thought they were unwieldy, but if you think there is vital info missing, let me know.
